questions.question: What is the main tradeoff when moving tests upward from unit to E2E?
options: Higher realism with higher runtime and maintenance cost
No tradeoff if CI is parallelized
questions.explanation: Higher-level tests usually capture more integrated behavior but cost more to run, diagnose, and stabilize.

questions.question: Which defect is most likely to escape a unit-heavy suite with weak integration coverage?
questions.explanation: Schema or contract mismatches often emerge at component boundaries, where isolated unit tests provide limited signal.

questions.question: A frontend-heavy app has high journey risk and moderate boundary risk. Which mix is usually most defensible?
options: Balanced integration-centric mix with thin critical-path E2E
No automated tests, manual QA only
questions.explanation: Journey-heavy systems need realistic interaction checks, but a thin E2E layer avoids high flake and cost while integration tests carry most realism.

questions.question: Why is flake rate a portfolio-level metric?
options: Because flakes only happen in unit tests
Because any flaky level can reduce trust in all pipeline signals
questions.explanation: Flaky failures consume triage time and train teams to ignore red pipelines, degrading the value of every testing level.

questions.question: What should drive test-level allocation first?
options: Number of engineers on the team
questions.explanation: A useful portfolio starts from the risks you need to control, then chooses the cheapest reliable evidence to cover those risks.

_meta.title: Unit, Integration, E2E - Pyramid vs Trophy
